summary: Jeff Atwood examines Bjarne Stroustrup's defense of C++ in a Technology Review interview and argues that the language's importance has diminished. While Stroustrup maintains that C++ is appropriately expert-friendly and that the problem is undereducated developers, Atwood counters that C++ made sense in an era of limited computing resources but is now overkill for most tasks. He argues that modern abundance of computing power means we should trade raw performance for developer safety and productivity. Atwood concludes that C++ remains valuable but is increasingly a niche language, and the most important quality of a modern language is how well it protects programmers from themselves.
key_points: C++ was designed for an era of scarce computing resources, but we now live in an era of abundance where naive solutions are often 'fast enough'
C++'s attempt to span all programming tasks from low-level to high-level makes it unnecessarily complex and dangerous for most modern development work
Stroustrup defends C++ by arguing developers are undereducated, but Atwood implies the real solution is using more appropriate, specialized languages
The most important quality of a programming language today isn't speed or generality, but how well it protects programmers from making mistakes
C++ has become a niche language suited only for specific tasks like operating systems and device drivers, not typical business applications
money_quotes: C++ is fast but unforgiving. It was an appropriate solution for an era of limited computing resources. But we've long since left that behind; we live in an era of abundance.
We have more computer power than we possibly know what to do with on the desktop. Computers get faster every day, but programmers' brains, sadly, do not.
It'd be a waste not to trade some of that abundant raw power to make things easier on us.
The most important question to ask about any language these days isn't how fast it is, or how general it is, but how well does it protect you from yourself?
C makes it easy to shoot yourself in the foot; C++ makes it harder, but when you do it blows your whole leg off.
There will always be a place in a programmer's toolbox for C++, but I'd argue that it's an increasingly a niche language for a very specific subset of programming tasks.
key_insight: In an era of abundant computing power, the most important quality of a programming language is no longer raw speed but how well it protects developers from making dangerous mistakes.
